title: Curry duck
subtitle: Trinidadian duck leg in Caribean curry
excerpt: Duck legs marinated overnight in green seasoning and curry powder, then slow-braised until the meat falls from the bone and the sauce reduces to a thick, fragrant, golden-brown gravy. Trinidadian curry duck is richer and more deeply flavoured than chicken curry, the duck fat bastes the meat throughout the braise and enriches the sauce. It is traditionally eaten at the beach, at fetes, and at Divali celebrations.
sourceNotes: Curry duck is one of the signature dishes of the Indian Trinidadian community, made in large quantities for outdoor celebrations, Divali, Phagwa, and the many food festivals that characterise Trinidadian culture. The overnight marinade in curry powder and green seasoning is standard practice. The dish appears in Trinidadian cookbooks from the mid-20th century and is one of the dishes that define Trinidadian cuisine internationally.

content.text: Duck legs render a significant amount of fat during cooking. After browning the duck, pour off the excess fat before adding the aromatics, otherwise the finished sauce will be very greasy. The fat that remains in the pan is enough to cook the onion and curry through.

The curry sauce should reduce and almost dry out by the end of cooking. The Trinidadian technique is to cook the duck until the sauce is thick enough that stirring it leaves a clear trail across the bottom of the pan. The duck fat that has rendered into the sauce gives it a richness and depth that cannot be achieved with chicken.

Combine the {{duck-leg}} with {{curry-powder}}, {{green-seasoning}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours. Heat a large heavy pot over high heat (no oil, the duck has its own fat). Brown the duck legs skin-side down for 5 to 6 minutes until deep golden and most of the fat has rendered. Flip and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Remove the duck. Pour off all but 2 tablespoons of the rendered fat.

Add the {{onion}} to the reserved fat and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{tomato}}, {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, and {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole). Cook for 3 minutes. Return the duck legs. Add 400 ml of water, bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer. Cover and cook for 1 hour. Remove the lid and cook for a further 20 to 30 minutes until the sauce has reduced and is thick enough to coat the duck deeply. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving. Serve with dhalpuri roti or white rice.

items.fix: Pour off the rendered fat after browning, keep only 2 tablespoons. If the sauce is already greasy, skim the top with a spoon or refrigerate and lift off the solidified fat
items.cause: Duck fat not drained after browning

items.fix: Duck legs need a long, gentle braise at barely a simmer. Continue cooking, the thigh meat will eventually become tender as the collagen breaks down
The overnight marinade is important. For more immediate flavour, add another tablespoon of curry powder to the pot when cooking the onion and allow it to cook for 2 minutes before adding the liquid
items.cause: Insufficient marinade time or curry powder not bloomed

content.text: Curry duck is one of the great Trinidadian outdoor foods. It is made in enormous pots at Divali nights, at the savannah, and at curry duck competitions held across the country. It is the dish that draws non-Indians into the Indian Trinidadian cooking tradition in the way that doubles does at street food level. Serve it with buss-up-shut roti or dhalpuri. The roti exists to scoop up the sauce.
